1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.WeilDivisor
9	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.OcOfD
10	
11	/-!
12	# The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.2** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
15	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
16	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
17	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
18	
19	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
20	through:
21	
22	- `RR.1` (`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` / `WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil
23	  divisor group `Div(C)` and the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
24	- **`RR.2` (this file, `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`)**: the Riemann–Roch
25	  dimension formula `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0` (with `deg D ≥ 0`),
26	  via the auxiliary Euler-characteristic identity
27	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`.
28	- `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, future): the invertible sheaf
29	  `𝒪_C(D)`, the linear-equivalence isomorphism `𝒪_C(D) ≅ 𝒪_C(D')` for
30	  `D ∼ D'`, and the `H¹`-vanishing input
31	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0` for `deg D ≥ 0` on a genus-`0` curve.
32	- `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34	
35	## Status (iter-174 Lane F file-skeleton)
36	
37	This file is the **iter-174 Lane F** file-skeleton: each of the four pinned
38	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
39	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). The
40	Euler-characteristic carrier definition is concrete (a one-line subtraction
41	of `H⁰` and `H¹` `Module.finrank`s, mirroring the `genus` definition of
42	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`); the remaining pins carry `sorry` bodies whose
43	closure is iter-175+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.3`
44	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`) and `RR.4` (`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
45	
46	The 4 pinned declarations are:
47	
48	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic` — Euler characteristic
49	   `χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)` of a `ModuleCat k̄`-valued
50	   sheaf on `C` (the curve specialisation of the alternating sum, since
51	   `H^i = 0` for `i ≥ 2` on a one-dimensional scheme by Grothendieck
52	   vanishing).
53	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l` — the `ℓ`-invariant
54	   `ℓ(D) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D))` of a Weil divisor `D`.
55	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
56	   — the Euler-characteristic identity `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` for
57	   every `D ∈ Div(C)` on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
58	   `C / k̄` of genus `g = g(C)`.
59	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero`
60	   — the Riemann–Roch formula in genus `0`: `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` for any
61	   Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)` with `deg D ≥ 0` on a smooth proper
62	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0` (threading
63	   the `H¹`-vanishing of `𝒪_C(D)` explicitly as a named premise until
64	   `RR.3` lands).
65	
66	## Note on `𝒪_C(D)` (the invertible sheaf of a divisor)
67	
68	The chapter's proof of `eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
69	and the statement of `l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero` both reference
70	the line bundle `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D`. Mathlib `b80f227` ships no
71	`Scheme.lineBundleOfDivisor` (the closest is `WeierstrassCurve.lineBundle`
72	in the elliptic-curve formalisation), and the project-side construction of
73	`𝒪_C(D)` is queued for `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). To keep the type
74	signatures of pins 2–4 substantive in the iter-174 skeleton, we expose a
75	**typed-`sorry` placeholder**
76	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` that pairs each divisor with
77	the `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf carrier `𝒪_C(D)` is intended to occupy. The
78	iter-175+ closure of `RR.3` replaces this placeholder's body with the
79	honest invertible-sheaf construction; the present pins consume it only
80	through its `H⁰` and `H¹` cohomology, so the consumer signatures are
81	substantive in the type sense (each asserts an arithmetic identity on the
82	finiteness-of-`H^*` outputs).
83

89	(Riemann–Roch) and Example 1.3.5 (genus-`0` specialisation). Stacks Project

101	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
102	
103	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
104	curve `C / k̄`.**
105	
106	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
107	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
108	alternating sum
109	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
110	expression
111	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
112	
113	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
114	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
115	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
116	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
117	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
118	
119	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
120	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
121	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
122	
123	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
124	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
125	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
126	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
127	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
128	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
129	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
130	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
131	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
132	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
133	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
134	
135	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (sibling chapter)
136	
137	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
138	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean`),
139	where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of closed points are glued
140	into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. **iter-183 Lane K** opened that file
141	with the typed-`sorry` pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` plus the three
142	immediate corollaries `sheafOf_zero`, `sheafOf_singlePoint`, and
143	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`. **iter-183 Lane H** (this file) retires the
144	former local `sheafOf` typed-`sorry` placeholder (previously at L168 in
145	the iter-174 skeleton) by importing `OcOfD.lean`; downstream pins (the
146	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) now
147	reference the chapter's canonical `sheafOf` directly. -/
148	
149	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
150	
151	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
152	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
153	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
154	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]

156	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
157	
158	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
159	`C / k̄`.**
160	
161	By definition,
162	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
163	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
164	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
165	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
166	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
167	of `genus C`.
168	
169	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
170	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
171	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
172	
173	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
174	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
175	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
176	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
177	
178	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
179	
180	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
181	
182	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
183	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
184	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
185	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
186	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
187	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
188	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
189	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
190	body.
191	
192	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base
193	case `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` and the inductive step `χ(𝒪_C(D + Y)) = χ(𝒪_C(D))
194	+ n`) intrinsically reference the body of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf`,
195	which is still a typed-`sorry` placeholder waiting on the sibling
196	chapter `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). We therefore factor the proof
197	into two named substantive helper lemmas (3-tier disclosure: **honest
198	named-sorry helpers** — each helper has a substantive type encoding a
199	nontrivial mathematical claim about the `sheafOf` line bundle whose
200	closure is downstream of the `RR.3` body):
201	
202	1. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` — base case `χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 − g`,
203	   which on closure of `RR.3` reduces to `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C`
204	   plus the Hartshorne I.3.4 input `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` plus the
205	   definition of the genus.
206	2. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` — inductive step
207	   `χ(sheafOf (Finsupp.single Y n + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + n` for any
208	   `Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor` and `n : ℤ`. On closure of `RR.3` this
209	   reduces to the Hartshorne IV.1.3 SES additivity argument iterated
210	   `|n|` times (with sign for `n < 0`).
211	
212	The main theorem then closes by induction on the `Finsupp`-structure of
213	`D : C.left.WeilDivisor` (via `Finsupp.induction`), using the two
214	helpers and the additivity of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`. -/
215	
216	/-- **Hartshorne I.3.4 bridge** (Lane H helper A — iter-183).
217	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the global
218	sections of the structure sheaf form a one-dimensional `k̄`-vector space:
219	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1`.
220	
221	This is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement "for any projective variety `X`
222	over an algebraically closed field `k`, `H⁰(X, 𝒪_X) ≅ k`" specialised to
223	the project's `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured cohomology pipeline
224	(`Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0`). The closure lives
225	in the project's `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` chapter (the H⁰
226	bridge from the constant-sheaf adjunction) and is gated on the
227	remaining cohomology-API work in that file.
228	
229	**iter-183 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. Body iter-184+
230	via the `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` H⁰-bridge. -/
231	private theorem Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one
232	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
233	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
234	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
235	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left] :
236	    Module.finrank kbar
237	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0) = 1 := by
238	  -- Step 1: H⁰-bridge LinearEquiv chain (existing project infrastructure).
239	  -- `HModule kbar (toModuleKSheaf C) 0` is identified, step by step, with
240	  -- the underlying global-sections ring `C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤)` via:
241	  --   `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`         -- Ext₀ ≃ Hom from constant sheaf;
242	  --   `constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv` -- constant-sheaf-Γ adjunction;
243	  --   `homFromOne_linearEquiv`            -- Hom-from-`k` evaluation at `1`;
244	  --   `SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top`     -- `Sheaf.Γ` reads the top section.
245	  let LE1 :=
246	    Scheme.HModule_zero_linearEquiv kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
247	  let LE2 :=
248	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom_linearEquiv kbar
249	      (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat) (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)
250	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
251	  let LE3 :=
252	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.homFromOne_linearEquiv kbar
253	      ((Sheaf.Γ (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
254	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C))
255	  let LE4 :=
256	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.SheafGammaObj_linearEquiv_top kbar
257	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)
258	  let LE := ((LE1.trans LE2).trans LE3).trans LE4
259	  rw [LE.finrank_eq]
260	  -- Step 2: `(toModuleKSheaf C).obj.obj (op ⊤)` is definitionally
261	  -- `ModuleCat.of kbar (C.left.presheaf.obj (op ⊤))` (per `toModuleKPresheaf_obj`),
262	  -- so we transport to the global-sections ring and apply
263	  -- `Module.finrank_of_bijective_algebraMap` + `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral`.
264	  -- Convert the ModuleCat coercion to the underlying global sections ring.
265	  change Module.finrank kbar
266	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
267	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) = 1
268	  -- Module-finiteness via the iter-044 Stein input.
269	  haveI hFin : Module.Finite kbar
270	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
271	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) :=
272	    AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_finite_globalSections_of_isProper kbar C
273	  -- Integrality of `C.left` propagates to the global sections being a domain
274	  -- (`IsIntegral.component_integral`), with the nonempty side-condition
275	  -- discharged from `IsIntegral.nonempty`.
276	  haveI hNECurve : Nonempty C.left.toTopCat :=
277	    AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral.nonempty
278	  -- `component_integral` requires `[Nonempty ↥↑U]` for the open `U = ⊤`; supply
279	  -- it inline via the curve nonemptiness, sidestepping the `↥↑` / `↑↑` coercion
280	  -- spelling mismatch that defeats `haveI`-based instance registration.
281	  haveI hDom : IsDomain
282	      (C.left.presheaf.obj
283	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))) :=
284	    @AlgebraicGeometry.IsIntegral.component_integral
285	      C.left _ (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)
286	      ⟨⟨hNECurve.some, Set.mem_univ _⟩⟩
287	  -- `Module.Finite kbar A` → `Algebra.IsIntegral kbar A` (auto-instance).
288	  -- Then `IsAlgClosed` + integrality + domain ⇒ algebraMap is bijective ⇒ finrank = 1.
289	  exact Module.finrank_of_bijective_algebraMap
290	    (IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral (k := kbar)
291	      (K := C.left.presheaf.obj
292	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))))
293	
294	/-- **χ-additivity on a SES of `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves** (Lane H
295	sub-helper — iter-186).
296	
297	For a short exact sequence `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` of sheaves on the
298	curve `C`, the Euler characteristic is additive:
299	`χ(S.X₂) = χ(S.X₁) + χ(S.X₃)`.
300	
301	**Proof outline (queued infrastructure).** Apply the project-side
302	`ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured covariant Ext-LES of Mathlib's
303	`Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` to `L = (constantSheaf J _).obj (ModuleCat.of
304	kbar kbar)`, getting the long exact sequence
305	`H⁰(F) → H⁰(G) → H⁰(H) → H¹(F) → H¹(G) → H¹(H) → H²(F) → H²(G) → H²(H)`.
306	Apply Grothendieck vanishing `H^i = 0` for `i ≥ 2` on a curve to truncate
307	to the 6-term form; rank-counting on a 6-term exact sequence of
308	finite-dimensional `kbar`-vector spaces gives the alternating identity
309	`finrank H⁰(F) − finrank H⁰(G) + finrank H⁰(H) − finrank H¹(F) +
310	finrank H¹(G) − finrank H¹(H) = 0`,
311	which rearranges to
312	`(finrank H⁰(G) − finrank H¹(G)) =
313	  (finrank H⁰(F) − finrank H¹(F)) + (finrank H⁰(H) − finrank H¹(H))`,
314	i.e. `χ(G) = χ(F) + χ(H)`.
315	
316	**iter-186 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The body depends
317	on (a) the project-side LES carrier for `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves
318	(downstream of `Cohomology/MayerVietorisCore.lean`), (b) Grothendieck
319	vanishing for `HModule kbar _ i` with `i ≥ 2` on a curve, and (c) the
320	6-term alternating-rank identity on `kbar`-finite-dimensional vector
321	spaces. -/

336	/-- **Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic** (Lane H sub-helper —
337	iter-186).
338	
339	If `F ≅ G` in `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`, then `χ(F) = χ(G)`. The proof
340	constructs an explicit `kbar`-linear equivalence
341	`HModule kbar F n ≃ₗ[kbar] HModule kbar G n` for each `n ∈ {0, 1}` via
342	`Abelian.Ext.postcompOfLinear` applied to `Ext.mk₀ e.hom` /
343	`Ext.mk₀ e.inv`; the mutual-inverse identities reduce, via
344	`comp_assoc_of_third_deg_zero` + `mk₀_comp_mk₀` + `Iso.hom_inv_id` /
345	`Iso.inv_hom_id` + `comp_mk₀_id`, to identities on `Ext` elements.
346	`LinearEquiv.finrank_eq` then transports the `kbar`-dimension. -/
347	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso
348	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
349	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
350	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
351	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
352	    {F G : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
353	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
354	    (e : F ≅ G) :
355	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C F = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C G := by
356	  -- The proof works uniformly at any cohomological degree `n`: build a
357	  -- `kbar`-linear equivalence `HModule kbar F n ≃ₗ[kbar] HModule kbar G n`.
358	  have hEquiv : ∀ n : ℕ, Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F n)
359	      = Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar G n) := by
360	    intro n
361	    have hEqv :
362	        Scheme.HModule kbar F n ≃ₗ[kbar] Scheme.HModule kbar G n := by
363	      refine LinearEquiv.ofLinear
364	        ((Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.hom).postcompOfLinear kbar _ (add_zero n))
365	        ((Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.inv).postcompOfLinear kbar _ (add_zero n))
366	        ?_ ?_
367	      · -- `f ∘ g = id` on `HModule kbar G n`: α ↦ α.comp (mk₀ e.inv).comp (mk₀ e.hom) = α.
368	        ext α
369	        change (α.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.inv) (add_zero n)).comp
370	            (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.hom) (add_zero n) = α
371	        rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc_of_third_deg_zero,
372	          Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, e.inv_hom_id]
373	        exact Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id _
374	      · -- `g ∘ f = id` on `HModule kbar F n`: α ↦ α.comp (mk₀ e.hom).comp (mk₀ e.inv) = α.
375	        ext α
376	        change (α.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.hom) (add_zero n)).comp
377	            (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ e.inv) (add_zero n) = α
378	        rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc_of_third_deg_zero,
379	          Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, e.hom_inv_id]
380	        exact Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id _
381	    exact hEqv.finrank_eq
382	  unfold Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
383	  rw [hEquiv 0, hEquiv 1]

402	**iter-186 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The body

405	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf
406	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
407	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
408	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]

411	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)] :
412	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C

417	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper
418	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
419	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
420	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
421	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
422	    (S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
423	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
424	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)))
425	    (_hSE : S.ShortExact)
426	    (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor)
427	    [∀ (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left), Decidable (P.point ∈ U)]
428	    (_h13 : Nonempty (S.X₃ ≅ skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
429	      P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar))) :
430	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C S.X₂
431	      = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C S.X₁ + 1 := by
432	  -- Assembly from the 3 substantive helpers:
433	  --   χ(X₂) = χ(X₁) + χ(X₃)               (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add)
434	  --        = χ(X₁) + χ(skyscraperSheaf …)  (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso via _h13)
435	  --        = χ(X₁) + 1                     (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf)
436	  obtain ⟨e⟩ := _h13
437	  have hAdd := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add C S _hSE
438	  have hIso := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso C e
439	  have hSky := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf C P
440	  rw [hAdd, hIso, hSky]
441	
442	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ
443	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
444	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
445	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
446	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
447	    (D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) (Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor) :
448	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
449	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y 1 + D))
450	      = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
451	          (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) + 1 := by
452	  classical
453	  -- Unpack the Hartshorne IV.1.3 closed-point SES from `RR.3`
454	  -- (Lane K typed-sorry `OcOfD.sheafOf_ses_single_add`):
455	  --   0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(single Y 1 + D) → k(Y) → 0
456	  obtain ⟨S, hSE, hX1, hX2, h13⟩ :=
457	    Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add (C := C) D Y
458	  -- Apply the packaged χ-additivity-with-skyscraper helper to convert the SES
459	  -- into an arithmetic identity at the χ level.
460	  have hχ :=
461	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_of_shortExact_skyscraper C S hSE Y h13
462	  -- Rewrite the X₁/X₂ identifications back to `sheafOf` to obtain the goal.
463	  rw [hX1, hX2] at hχ
464	  exact hχ
465	
466	/-- **Base case of the χ-identity** (iter-183 Lane H): on a smooth
467	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the Euler characteristic
468	of the structure-sheaf-side line bundle `sheafOf 0` is `1 − g(C)`.
469	
470	The proof rewrites `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C` (the chapter
471	`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex` `sheafOf_zero` lemma), unfolds the
472	two-term `eulerCharacteristic`, and combines `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1`
473	(`Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one`) with the definitional
474	unfold `genus C = dim H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.
475	
476	**iter-183 Lane H status** — body assembled axiom-clean modulo
477	`OcOfD.sheafOf_zero` (Lane K typed sorry) and the H⁰ bridge typed
478	sorry; both gated on iter-184+ infrastructure. -/
479	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero
480	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
481	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
482	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
483	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left] :
484	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
485	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (0 : C.left.WeilDivisor))
486	      = 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) := by
487	  rw [Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero (C := C)]
488	  unfold Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
489	  rw [Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one C]
490	  simp [AlgebraicGeometry.genus]
491	
492	/-- **Inductive step of the χ-identity** (iter-183 Lane H): on a smooth
493	proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the Euler characteristic
494	of `sheafOf` transports across the elementary modification
495	`D ↦ Finsupp.single Y n + D` with arithmetic increment `n`. The argument
496	`D` carries the underlying `Finsupp` type rather than `Scheme.WeilDivisor`
497	so that the `Finsupp.single Y n + D` term elaborates cleanly; the result
498	is consumed via the definitional equality `WeilDivisor = (PrimeDivisor →₀
499	ℤ)`.
500	
501	The body inducts on `n : ℤ` via `Int.induction_on`:
502	- `n = 0`: `Finsupp.single Y 0 = 0`, so `single Y 0 + D = D`.
503	- `n = k + 1` (positive direction): rewrite
504	  `single Y (k+1) + D = single Y 1 + (single Y k + D)`
505	  via `Finsupp.single_add`, apply
506	  `Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ`, then the inductive
507	  hypothesis.
508	- `n = -(k+1)` (negative direction): apply
509	  `Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ` to
510	  `D' := single Y (-(k+1)) + D` and simplify; the LHS `single Y 1 + D'`
511	  rewrites to `single Y (-k) + D` via `Finsupp.single_add` and
512	  arithmetic, giving the identity backwards from the inductive
513	  hypothesis at `-k`.
514	
515	**iter-183 Lane H status** — body sorry-free assembly modulo the
516	`_succ` typed-sorry helper. Once `_succ` closes iter-184+, this lemma
517	upgrades to Tier-1. -/
518	private theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add
519	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
520	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
521	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
522	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
523	    (D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) (Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
524	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
525	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y n + D))
526	      = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
527	          (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) + n := by
528	  refine Int.induction_on (motive := fun m : ℤ =>
529	      Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
530	          (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y m + D))
531	        = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
532	            (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) + m) n ?_ ?_ ?_
533	  · -- n = 0
534	    simp
535	  · -- positive step: motive (↑k) → motive (↑k + 1)
536	    intro k ih
537	    have hsplit :
538	        (Finsupp.single Y ((k : ℤ) + 1) + D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
539	          = Finsupp.single Y 1 + (Finsupp.single Y (k : ℤ) + D) := by
540	      rw [show ((k : ℤ) + 1) = (1 + (k : ℤ)) from by ring,
541	        Finsupp.single_add, add_assoc]
542	    have hstep := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ
543	      C (Finsupp.single Y (k : ℤ) + D) Y
544	    have h1 : Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
545	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C)
546	          (Finsupp.single Y ((k : ℤ) + 1) + D))
547	        = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
548	            (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C)
549	              (Finsupp.single Y 1 + (Finsupp.single Y (k : ℤ) + D))) :=
550	      congrArg (fun e => Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
551	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) e)) hsplit
552	    linarith [h1, hstep, ih]
553	  · -- negative step: motive (-↑k) → motive (-↑k - 1)
554	    intro k ih
555	    have hsplit :
556	        (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ)) + D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
557	          = Finsupp.single Y 1 +
558	              (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ) - 1) + D) := by
559	      rw [← add_assoc, ← Finsupp.single_add]
560	      congr 2
561	      ring
562	    have hpred := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_succ
563	      C (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ) - 1) + D) Y
564	    -- hpred: χ(sheafOf (single Y 1 + (single Y (-k-1) + D))) = χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k-1) + D)) + 1
565	    -- Combine via hsplit and congr 1 to bridge dot-notation vs full-name display:
566	    have key : Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
567	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ)) + D))
568	        = Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
569	            (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C)
570	              (Finsupp.single Y 1 + (Finsupp.single Y (-(k : ℤ) - 1) + D))) :=
571	      congrArg (fun e => Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C
572	        (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) e)) hsplit
573	    -- ih: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k) + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + (-k)
574	    -- key: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k) + D)) = χ(sheafOf (single Y 1 + (single Y (-k - 1) + D)))
575	    -- hpred: χ(sheafOf (s Y 1 + (s Y (-k-1) + D))) = χ(sheafOf (s Y (-k-1) + D)) + 1
576	    -- Goal: χ(sheafOf (single Y (-k - 1) + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + (-k - 1)
577	    linarith [key, hpred, ih]
578	
579	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
580	of genus `g`.**
581	
582	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
583	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
584	
585	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
586	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
587	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
588	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
589	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
590	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.
591	
592	Blueprint reference: `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
593	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295).
594	
595	**Iter-181 Lane H status** — the main theorem is closed by induction on
596	the `Finsupp` structure of `D`, consuming the two `sheafOf`-side helper
597	sorries `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` (base case) and
598	`eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` (inductive step). The body
599	itself is honest; its `sorryAx`-transitivity is the inevitable
600	consequence of `sheafOf` still being a typed sorry waiting on `RR.3`. -/
601	theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
602	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
603	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
604	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
605	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
606	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
607	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D)
608	      = (Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D) + 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) := by
609	  -- Unfold `WeilDivisor` to expose the underlying `Finsupp` structure so
610	  -- `Finsupp.induction` applies directly.
611	  unfold Scheme.WeilDivisor at D
612	  induction D using Finsupp.induction with
613	  | zero =>
614	    -- D = 0: χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 - g by helper 1, and degree 0 = 0.
615	    exact (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero C).trans
616	      (by simp [Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree])
617	  | single_add Y n D' _hY _hn hD' =>
618	    -- D = single Y n + D': use helper 2 then the inductive hypothesis.
619	    rw [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add, hD']
620	    -- Goal: (degree D' + 1 - g) + n = degree (single Y n + D') + 1 - g.
621	    have hdeg : Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree
622	        ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.WeilDivisor))
623	        = n + Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D' := by
624	      change ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)).sum
625	          (fun _ z => z) = _
626	      classical
627	      rw [Finsupp.sum_add_index (fun _ _ => rfl) (fun _ _ _ _ => rfl)]
628	      simp [Finsupp.sum_single_index]
629	      rfl
630	    linarith
631	
632	/-! ## §5. The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero -/
633	
634	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
635	
636	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
637	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
638	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
639	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
640	
641	The proof specialises the χ-identity
642	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
643	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
644	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
645	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
646	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
647	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
648	
649	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
650	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
651	theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
652	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
653	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
654	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
655	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
656	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
657	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
658	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
659	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :
660	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.l (C := C) D : ℤ)
661	      = Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D + 1 := by
662	  have h := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus C D
663	  simp only [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic, _hg, _hH1,
664	    Nat.cast_zero, sub_zero] at h
665	  exact h
666	
667	end AlgebraicGeometry

3	\chapter{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero (RR.2)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, IV \S1, pp.~294--297
7	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages
8	% 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below
9	% were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of
10	% the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter is the second of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
15	that close the project's headline RR bridge
16	\[
17	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
18	  \,\colon\,
19	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
20	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
21	\]
22	The bridge consumed by
23	REF of
24	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} is the Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain:
25	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) and the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
26	\to \mathbb Z$ (\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the
27	Riemann--Roch dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
28	\[
29	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
30	\]
31	(this chapter, \texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections identification
32	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (\texttt{RR.3}); the
33	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
34	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
35	
36	% NOTE (Serre duality vs. direct \(\chi\) — iter-173 sub-phase choice).
37	% This chapter proves the RR formula via the \emph{direct \(\chi\)
38	% inductive proof} (Hartshorne IV.1.3 step ``Next, let \(D\) be any
39	% divisor\ldots'', p.~296), \emph{not} via Serre duality. The reason is
40	% strictly Mathlib-side: Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships no
41	% dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and no Serre duality theorem
42	% \(H^i(X, \mathcal F) \cong H^{n-i}(X, \omega_X \otimes \mathcal F^\vee)^*\);
43	% routing through that machinery would force a multi-iter sub-build
44	% strictly heavier than the genus-\(0\) critical path requires. The
45	% direct \(\chi\) proof depends only on additivity of the Euler
46	% characteristic on a short exact sequence of coherent sheaves (a
47	% standard preadditive-category fact), the genus base case
48	% \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\), and the closed-point skyscraper
49	% identity \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\).
50	
51	% NOTE (Genus-\(0\) carve-out vs. general \(g\) — iter-173 sub-phase
52	% choice). The chapter restricts the headline theorem
53	% REF to the genus-\(0\) specialisation
54	% \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). The general-\(g\)
55	% statement \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) is downstream of
56	% \texttt{RR.2} (it requires the canonical divisor \(K\) and Serre
57	% duality / the \(K - D\) pairing) and is \emph{not} needed for the
58	% genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.3} consumes only
59	% REF, and \texttt{RR.4} consumes
60	% \texttt{RR.3}. The intermediate \(\chi\)-identity
61	% REF is stated and
62	% sketched for general \(g\) (the inductive argument is genus-uniform);
63	% its full closure on a general-\(g\) curve waits for the project's
64	% subsequent Riemann--Roch chapter, but the proof body in
65	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} pins the
66	% \emph{statement} for general \(g\) and may close the body initially
67	% under a \(g = 0\) hypothesis.
68	
69	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
70	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
71	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
72	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}) carry the same
73	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
74	\begin{quote}
75	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
76	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
77	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
78	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
79	\end{quote}
80	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
81	\texttt{Genus.tex}, namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\)
82	via the project's \(\Module\bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
83	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}).
84	
85	\section{The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve}
86	
87	\begin{definition}
88	
89	
90	\leanok
91	[Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve]
92	  \label{def:eulerChar_curve}
93	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic}
94	  
95	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(\chi(\mathcal F)\)
96	  % inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
97	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
98	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
99	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
100	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
101	  % is the Euler characteristic
102	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
103	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295, the formula displayed
104	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3.}
105	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F\)
106	  be a coherent sheaf on \(C\). The \emph{Euler characteristic} of
107	  \(\mathcal F\) is the integer
108	  \[
109	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;:=\;
110	    \sum_{i \geq 0} (-1)^i \,\dim_{\bar k} H^i(C, \mathcal F)
111	    \;=\;
112	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal F)
113	    \,-\,
114	    \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal F).
115	  \]
116	  The second equality is a curve-specific simplification: because
117	  \(\dim C = 1\), Grothendieck vanishing (Hartshorne~III.2.7) gives
118	  \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\), so the alternating sum has
119	  only two terms. Coherence of \(\mathcal F\) on the proper $\bar
120	  k\(-scheme \)C\( guarantees that both \)H^0\( and \)H^1$ are
121	  finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology
122	  finiteness theorem, the same finiteness backing
123	  REF), so the two terms are honest natural numbers and
124	  the difference is a well-defined integer.
125	
126	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
127	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic} takes a coherent
128	  sheaf \(\mathcal F\) on \(C\) and returns an integer; the natural input
129	  shape is the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
130	  wrapper \(\HModule \bar k\) (REF) applied to a
131	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured carrier built from \(\mathcal F\), with
132	  \(\dim_{\bar k}\) extracted via \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\). The
133	  declaration is \texttt{noncomputable} (inherited from
134	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) and from the \(\Ext\)-based cohomology
135	  construction).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\section{The \(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor}
139	
140	\begin{definition}
141	
142	
143	\leanok
144	[\(\ell\)-invariant of a Weil divisor]
145	  \label{def:l_invariant}
146	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l}
147	  
148	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\) and of the
149	  % "dimension of \(|D|\)") (read from
150	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
151	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We denote \(\dim_k H^0(X, \mathscr L(D))\) by \(l(D)\),
152	  % so that the \emph{dimension of \(|D|\)} is \(l(D) - 1\). The number
153	  % \(l(D)\) is finite by (II, 5.19) or (III, 5.2)."
154	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~295 (definition of \(l(D)\)).}
155	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let
156	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor (REF). The
157	  \emph{\(\ell\)-invariant of \(D\)} is the integer
158	  \[
159	    \ell(D) \;:=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
160	    \;\in\; \mathbb N,
161	  \]
162	  the \(\bar k\)-dimension of the space of global sections of the
163	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\). Finiteness of
164	  \(\ell(D)\) is a consequence of the coherent-cohomology finiteness
165	  theorem on a proper \(\bar k\)-scheme (Hartshorne~II.5.19 or
166	  III.5.2). The classical \emph{complete linear system \(|D|\)} has
167	  \(\bar k\)-dimension \(\ell(D) - 1\) as a projective space, but the
168	  chapter never uses the projective-space interpretation; \(\ell(D)\) is
169	  the only quantity it needs.
170	
171	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
172	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l} accepts a divisor
173	  \(D : C.\mathtt{WeilDivisor}\) (REF) and returns
174	  \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\;(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)))\),
175	  computed through the same \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf-cohomology
176	  pipeline as REF. The associated invertible
177	  sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is the line bundle of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
178	  chapter, to be added); the present chapter's prose and Lean
179	  signature reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) by name and consume only its
180	  \(H^0\) functor. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
181	\end{definition}
182	
183	\section{The \(\chi\) identity: \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\)}
184	
185	\subsection*{Substrate lemmas for the proof of the \(\chi\)-identity}
186	
187	The proof of REF below
188	decomposes into three substantive substrate lemmas, factored out of the
189	Hartshorne~IV.1.3 inductive argument. The first
190	(REF, \emph{additivity}) is the
191	classical statement that \(\chi\) descends to the Grothendieck group
192	of \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\); the second
193	(REF, \emph{iso-invariance}) is the
194	elementary fact that \(\chi\) is well defined on isomorphism classes
195	of sheaves; the third
196	(REF, \emph{skyscraper}) computes \(\chi\)
197	on a closed-point skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\). Pinning each as its own
198	Lean declaration lets the prover attack the three substrate gaps
199	independently of the parent inductive argument.
200	
201	\begin{lemma}\leanok
202	[Additivity of the Euler characteristic on a short exact sequence]
203	  \label{lem:euler_char_shortExact_add}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add}
205	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
206	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (inside the proof of Theorem~1.3,
207	  % the citation ``III, Ex.~5.1'' for the additivity of \(\chi\) on a
208	  % short exact sequence) (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
211	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
212	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (citation of III, Ex.~5.1
214	  inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
216	  \(\bar k\), and let
217	  \[
218	    0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0
219	  \]
220	  be a short exact sequence in the category of \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
221	  sheaves on \(C\). Then
222	  \[
223	    \chi(\mathcal F_2) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3).
224	  \]
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{proof}
228	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
229	  Apply the project-side \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured covariant Ext-LES
230	  (Mathlib's \texttt{Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence}) to the constant-sheaf
231	  test object \(L = (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
232	  \bar k\;\bar k)\) and the short exact sequence
233	  \(0 \to \mathcal F_1 \to \mathcal F_2 \to \mathcal F_3 \to 0\). The
234	  resulting nine-term sequence
235	  \[
236	    H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to H^0(\mathcal F_3)
237	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2) \to H^1(\mathcal F_3)
238	    \to H^2(\mathcal F_1) \to H^2(\mathcal F_2) \to H^2(\mathcal F_3)
239	  \]
240	  is exact in the category of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces. Grothendieck
241	  vanishing \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i \geq 2\) on the
242	  one-dimensional scheme \(C\) (Hartshorne~III.2.7) truncates the
243	  sequence to a six-term exact sequence
244	  \[
245	    0 \to K_0 \to H^0(\mathcal F_1) \to H^0(\mathcal F_2) \to
246	    H^0(\mathcal F_3) \to H^1(\mathcal F_1) \to H^1(\mathcal F_2)
247	    \to H^1(\mathcal F_3) \to 0
248	  \]
249	  (with \(K_0 = 0\) the kernel of the first map, kept here only as a
250	  bookkeeping placeholder). Rank-counting on a six-term exact sequence
251	  of finite-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector spaces — iterated applications
252	  of \(\mathtt{Submodule.finrank\_quotient\_add\_finrank}\) at each
253	  intermediate kernel/image quotient — yields the alternating identity
254	  \[
255	    \dim H^0(\mathcal F_1) - \dim H^0(\mathcal F_2) + \dim H^0(\mathcal F_3)
256	    - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_1) + \dim H^1(\mathcal F_2) - \dim H^1(\mathcal F_3)
257	    \;=\; 0,
258	  \]
259	  which rearranges, using the definition
260	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
261	  (REF), to
262	  \(\chi(\mathcal F_2) = \chi(\mathcal F_1) + \chi(\mathcal F_3)\).
263	
264	  % NOTE: gated on project-side LES carrier + finiteness machinery.
265	  % Mathlib `b80f227` ships `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` but not (a)
266	  % the `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured LES carrier specialised to the
267	  % project's sheaf-cohomology pipeline `Scheme.HModule`, (b) Grothendieck
268	  % vanishing `H^i(C, F) = 0` for `i ≥ 2` at the `Ext`-cohomology level
269	  % consumed by `HModule`, nor (c) the six-term rank-counting bridge
270	  % composed of `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` invocations.
271	  % The Lean helper `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` therefore
272	  % carries a Tier-3 honest typed sorry; its closure lands once the
273	  % three substrate pieces are in place. Off the genus-0 critical path
274	  % (the parent theorem `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
275	  % is the only consumer in `RR.2`).
276	\end{proof}
277	
278	\begin{lemma}\leanok
279	[Iso-invariance of the Euler characteristic]
280	  \label{lem:euler_char_iso}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_iso}
282	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
283	  \textit{Project-bespoke decomposition} (Archon-original packaging of
284	  the Mathlib \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; no external source named).
285	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
286	  \(\bar k\) and let \(\mathcal F, \mathcal G\) be \(\Module \bar k\)-valued
287	  sheaves on \(C\) with an isomorphism \(\mathcal F \cong \mathcal G\).
288	  Then
289	  \[
290	    \chi(\mathcal F) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal G).
291	  \]
292	\end{lemma}
293	
294	\begin{proof}
295	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
296	  \leanok
297	  An isomorphism \(e \colon \mathcal F \xrightarrow{\;\cong\;} \mathcal G\)
298	  induces, in each cohomological degree \(n \in \mathbb N\), a
299	  \(\bar k\)-linear equivalence
300	  \[
301	    H^n(C, \mathcal F) \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\; H^n(C, \mathcal G)
302	  \]
303	  by post-composition with the Ext-degree-zero classes
304	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{hom})\) and
305	  \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0(e.\mathtt{inv})\). The mutual-inverse identities
306	  for this pair of post-compositions reduce — via the associativity
307	  lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.comp\_assoc\_of\_third\_deg\_zero}\), the
308	  composition lemma \(\mathtt{Ext.mk}_0\mathtt{\_comp\_mk}_0\), and the
309	  iso identities \(e.\mathtt{hom} \circ e.\mathtt{inv} = \mathrm{id}\),
310	  \(e.\mathtt{inv} \circ e.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}\) — to the identity
311	  map on \(\Ext\) elements. Specialising to \(n = 0\) and \(n = 1\) and
312	  applying \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) at each of those two
313	  degrees gives
314	  \(\dim H^n(C, \mathcal F) = \dim H^n(C, \mathcal G)\) for
315	  \(n \in \{0, 1\}\). Unfolding the definition
316	  \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\)
317	  (REF) closes the goal.
318	\end{proof}
319	
320	\begin{lemma}\leanok
321	[Euler characteristic of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf]
322	  \label{lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
323	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_skyscraperSheaf}
324	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
325	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) input
326	  % cited inline inside the proof of Theorem~1.3) (read from
327	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
328	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
329	  % exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1), and \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), so we have
330	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\)."
331	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1 p.~296 (the ``\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\)''
332	  input inside the proof of Theorem~1.3).}
333	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
334	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Write \(k(P)\) for
335	  the closed-point skyscraper sheaf
336	  \[
337	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
338	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),
339	  \]
340	  the \(\Module \bar k\)-valued sheaf on \(C\) with stalk \(\bar k\) at
341	  \(P\) and zero stalks elsewhere. Then
342	  \[
343	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; 1.
344	  \]
345	\end{lemma}
346	
347	\begin{proof}
348	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve}
349	  By the definition of \(\chi\)
350	  (REF),
351	  \[
352	    \chi(k(P)) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P))
353	    \;-\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)).
354	  \]
355	  The closure decomposes cleanly into two independent half-statements,
356	  attacked separately by the prover.
357	
358	  \emph{H\(^0\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\).
359	  Compose the project's three-step \(\bar k\)-linear-equivalence chain
360	  \[
361	    H^0(C, k(P))
362	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
363	    \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Sh}}\!\bigl(
364	      (\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k),\,
365	      k(P)
366	    \bigr)
367	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
368	    \Gamma(C, k(P))
369	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\cong\;}\;
370	    (k(P))(C),
371	  \]
372	  realised by the project lemmas
373	  \texttt{Scheme.HModule\_zero\_linearEquiv},
374	  \texttt{Scheme.constantSheafGammaHom\_linearEquiv}, and
375	  \texttt{Scheme.SheafGammaObj\_linearEquiv\_top} (the last step
376	  identifies the abstract \(\Gamma\) functor with the underlying
377	  presheaf evaluation at the top open \(\top\), i.e.\ at the whole
378	  underlying space of \(C\)). Applying
379	  \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.finrank\_eq}\) along this chain reduces the
380	  goal to
381	  \(\dim_{\bar k} (k(P))(C) = 1\). The closed point
382	  \(P\) lies in the top open of \(C\), so the skyscraper presheaf
383	  evaluates at the top open to its stalk
384	  \(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k\) by definition, and
385	  \(\dim_{\bar k} \bar k = 1\) closes the half. This half is
386	  axiom-clean closure projection (\(\sim 30\text{--}60\) LOC) against
387	  the existing project chain; it is the natural target for the
388	  iteration that follows the present blueprint expansion.
389	
390	  \emph{H\(^1\) half} — \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
391	  Mathematically, the closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque (any
392	  pushforward of a sheaf from a one-point space is flasque;
393	  Hartshorne~III.2.5(c)), and flasque sheaves have vanishing higher
394	  cohomology in degrees \(\geq 1\) on any topological space
395	  (Hartshorne~III.2.5). Hence \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) as a
396	  \(\bar k\)-vector space.
397	
398	  % NOTE: gated on Mathlib flasque cohomology.
399	  % Mathlib `b80f227` does NOT ship an `H^n(X, F) = 0 for flasque F`
400	  % lemma at the `ModuleCat`-flavoured `Abelian.Ext`-cohomology level
401	  % used by the project's `Scheme.HModule kbar` pipeline. The closure
402	  % depends on either (a) an upstream Mathlib
403	  % `Sheaf.flasque_vanishing_cohomology` contribution at the
404	  % `Ext`-cohomology level for sheaves with values in an abelian
405	  % category with enough injectives, or (b) a project-side flasque-
406	  % cohomology bridge (~150-300 LOC, off the genus-0 critical path).
407	  % Until one of the two lands, the H¹ half is a typed-sorry blocker
408	  % independent of the H⁰ half — i.e. the prover can attack the H⁰
409	  % half against the existing project chain without waiting for the
410	  % H¹ machinery, and the present skyscraper lemma waits for the H¹
411	  % gap to clear.
412	
413	  Combining the two halves through the definition of \(\chi\),
414	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1 - 0 = 1\), closes the lemma.
415	\end{proof}
